name: Birth Plan Decisions
description: Key labor and delivery decisions to discuss with your partner and OB before the big day

items.item: Decide on unmedicated vs medicated labor
items.details: Talk through pain tolerance honestly. No medals for suffering - pick what works for your partner.
Upright, hands-and-knees, side-lying, squatting - your partner should know the options before labor starts.

items.item: Discuss water birth or laboring in tub
items.details: Not all hospitals offer this. Confirm availability now so there's no day-of disappointment.
Walking the halls, bouncing on a birth ball, swaying - continuous monitoring may limit options.

items.item: Decide on intermittent vs continuous fetal monitoring
items.details: Continuous keeps you tethered to the bed. Ask your OB if intermittent is an option for low-risk pregnancies.
Some want it early, some want to wait. Know that you can request one at almost any point during labor.
Not every hospital has it. It takes the edge off without full numbness - worth knowing if it's an option.
Take a class or watch videos together. Even with an epidural, breathing helps during early labor and pushing.
Fentanyl or Stadol can help in early labor. They make you drowsy and cross to baby, so timing matters.

items.item: Decide who will be in the delivery room
items.details: Partner, doula, family members - set this boundary now. Most hospitals limit to 2-3 support people.
Decide what's okay to capture. Some hospitals prohibit video during delivery itself - ask in advance.
Make a playlist, bring a speaker. Most rooms have dimmable lights - a calmer environment helps.

items.item: Decide if dad wants to cut the cord
items.details: No pressure either way. The nurse will guide you through it if you do. It's tougher than you'd think - like cutting a garden hose.
Waiting 1-3 minutes lets extra blood flow to baby. AAP recommends it. Put it in your plan and remind the team.
Baby goes directly on mom's chest. If mom can't, dad does skin-to-skin. Tell the nurses this is your preference.
Private banking costs $1,500-2,500 upfront plus $150-300/year storage. Public donation is free. Decide by week 34.
Request that non-urgent newborn procedures (weighing, vitamin K, eye ointment) wait until after the first hour of skin-to-skin.
Breastfeeding within the first hour boosts success rates. Even if formula-feeding, that first latch attempt matters.
If it goes to C-section: who stays in the OR, does dad want to watch, clear drape vs standard. Have this conversation before labor.

items.item: NICU plan if baby needs extra care
items.details: Who stays with baby, who stays with mom. Know where the NICU is in your hospital. Ask about parent access policies.
If there's hemorrhaging, the team needs to act fast. Discuss any religious or personal objections with your OB now, not in the moment.
Vacuum or forceps may be needed if pushing stalls. Know the risks and tell your OB your preferences in advance.

name: Nursery Setup
description: Everything you need to build a safe, functional nursery before baby arrives

items.item: Assemble crib and check for recalls
items.details: Check cpsc.gov for recalls on your model. Slat spacing must be under 2-3/8 inches. No drop-side cribs - they're banned.

items.item: Set up changing table or dresser-top changer
items.details: Waist height saves your back. Secure the changing pad with straps - babies roll sooner than you think.

items.item: Get a glider or rocker for night feeds
items.details: You'll log 200+ hours in this chair the first year. Test before buying - comfort beats style.

items.item: Set up dresser for baby clothes
items.details: Organize by size and type. Pro tip: dividers with labels save 3 AM fumbling in the dark.

items.item: Anchor all furniture to the wall
items.details: Dressers, bookshelves, changing table - use anti-tip straps. A dresser falling is the #1 nursery danger. Takes 10 minutes per piece.

items.item: Install cord covers or cord cleats
items.details: Blinds cords are a strangulation risk. Switch to cordless blinds or secure cords out of reach with cleats.
Use sliding outlet covers (not plug-in caps, which are choking hazards). Cover every outlet within 3 feet of the floor.
One smoke detector in the nursery, CO detector on the same floor. Test them monthly. Replace batteries every 6 months.
Even a sliver of light can wreck a nap. Overlap curtains past the window frame or use Velcro strips for full coverage.

items.item: Set up a white noise machine
items.details: Place it at least 7 feet from the crib at under 50 decibels. Continuous white noise beats heartbeat or wave sounds for most babies.
Video monitors with night vision are worth it. Mount it out of reach, at least 3 feet from the crib. No cords near the crib.

items.item: Set up a dim night light
items.details: Red or amber light only - blue and white light suppress melatonin. You need just enough light for diaper changes.
Ideal nursery temp is 68-72°F. Babies can't regulate body temp well - overheating increases SIDS risk.
Diapers, wipes, cream, bags for dirty diapers, hand sanitizer - all within one-arm reach. You'll change 10-12 diapers a day.
Newborn, 0-3, 3-6 months in separate sections. Remove tags and wash everything in fragrance-free detergent before baby arrives.

items.item: Place a laundry hamper in the nursery
items.details: You'll generate a shocking amount of tiny laundry. A hamper in the room means fewer trips with spit-up clothes.
Infant Tylenol, gas drops, saline drops, nasal aspirator, thermometer, nail clippers. Keep it in a labeled bin on the shelf.

items.item: Set up a feeding station in the nursery
items.details: Water bottle, burp cloths, nursing pillow, phone charger - everything you need during 2 AM feeds in one spot.
High-contrast black and white patterns are best for newborn vision. Save the pastel stuff for 3+ months.
Mount securely out of reach. Remove it once baby can push up on hands and knees (around 5 months).
Board books from day one. Reading to your newborn builds neural connections even if they can't understand a word.
Soft surface for tummy time later. Make sure it's non-slip and machine washable - it will get bodily fluids on it.

name: Car Seat Installation
description: Step-by-step guide to getting the car seat installed correctly - the hospital won't let you leave without it

items.details: Rear-facing until at least age 2 or until they outgrow the height/weight limit. Convertible seats last longer but infant carriers are easier to carry.
Not every seat fits every car. Check the seat manufacturer's fit guide and measure your back seat before buying.

items.item: Register the car seat with the manufacturer
items.details: Takes 2 minutes online. If there's ever a recall, they'll contact you directly instead of you finding out by accident.

items.item: Check the manufacture and expiration date
items.details: Car seats expire 6-10 years from manufacture date. Never use a secondhand seat if you don't know its full history.

items.item: Choose LATCH or seatbelt installation method
items.details: LATCH is easier but has a weight limit (usually 65 lbs combined child + seat). Seatbelt works for any weight. Never use both at the same time.

items.item: Install the base at the correct angle
items.details: Most bases have a built-in level indicator. Newborns need a more reclined angle (30-45 degrees) to keep their airway open.
Grab the base where the seatbelt or LATCH connects and pull hard. It should not move more than 1 inch side to side or front to back.
The level bubble or line should be in the green zone. If not, use a rolled towel under the base to adjust. Recheck after adjusting.
For rear-facing, harness straps go through slots at or below baby's shoulders. Chest clip sits at armpit level.

items.item: Do the pinch test on harness straps
items.details: Tighten straps, then try to pinch the strap at baby's shoulder. If you can pinch a fold of webbing, it's too loose.

items.item: Schedule a fire station or police inspection
items.details: Free car seat checks are available at most fire stations and some police departments. Call ahead - not all locations have certified technicians on duty.

items.item: Read the car seat manual cover to cover
items.details: Every seat is different. YouTube videos are great but your specific manual is the final authority on installation for your model.

items.item: Read your vehicle owner's manual car seat section
items.details: Your car may have specific LATCH anchor locations or seatbelt lock-off features you don't know about. Check pages on child restraints.

items.item: Practice buckling in a baby-sized doll or stuffed animal
items.details: Use a 7-8 lb stuffed animal to practice strapping in. Get the harness adjustment and chest clip placement muscle memory down.

items.item: Practice removing and reinstalling the carrier from the base
items.details: You'll do this dozens of times. The click-in and release mechanism should be second nature before baby arrives.

items.item: Time yourself on a full install and removal
items.details: You should be able to install the base from scratch in under 10 minutes. If you can't, practice more or get it inspected.
Clips to the rear headrest so you can see baby's face in your rearview mirror. Essential for rear-facing peace of mind.

items.item: Add window shades on rear windows
items.details: Static cling shades block sun without suction cups falling off. Keeps baby cool and prevents sunburn on the drive home.
Extra diapers, wipes, change of clothes, blanket, plastic bags in a small bin in the trunk. You will need this sooner than you think.

items.item: Remove any loose objects from the rear shelf and back seat
items.details: In a sudden stop, a water bottle becomes a projectile. Clear the back seat area completely around the car seat.

name: First Solo Outing with Baby
description: Everything you need for your first trip out of the house alone with baby - you've got this

items.details: You'll probably use 2-3, but blowouts happen in pairs. Better to carry extra than sprint to a store one-handed.
Wipes clean everything - hands, face, surfaces, blowout situations. Never go anywhere with a half-empty pack.
One for the expected spit-up, one for the unexpected blowout. Full outfits including socks.
Ziplock bags or wet bags. Nothing worse than a poop-covered onesie loose in your bag contaminating everything.
Not every restroom has a changing table. A foldable pad lets you change baby on any flat surface hygienically.

items.item: Pack bottles and formula if bottle-feeding
items.details: Bring one more bottle than you think you need. Pre-measured formula dispenser is a game changer for outings.
Cloth diapers work great as burp cloths. Drape one over your shoulder before every feed - saves your shirt.

items.item: Bring a nursing cover if breastfeeding in public
items.details: Totally optional - feed however you're comfortable. But having one in the bag gives you the choice.
Pacifiers fall on the ground constantly. Bring 2 and a pacifier clip. The clip saves you from the floor-pacifier-panic cycle.
Something familiar that smells like home. It can buy you 5 minutes of calm in a pinch.

items.item: Light blanket and an extra swaddle
items.details: For warmth, shade, nursing cover backup, stroller cover, or emergency spit-up cleanup. Blankets are Swiss army knives.

items.item: Plan your route with potential stops
items.details: Know where the restrooms and changing stations are along your route. Google Maps reviews often mention family-friendly facilities.

items.item: Locate changing stations at your destination
items.details: Not every bathroom has one. Check the family restroom or ask guest services. Worst case, your car trunk and a changing pad work.

items.item: Save emergency contacts in your phone favorites
items.details: Pediatrician, partner, and one backup person on speed dial. You need to be able to call one-handed while holding baby.

items.item: Do a car seat check before you leave
items.details: Harness snug, chest clip at armpit level, nothing loose in the back seat. Takes 30 seconds and it's non-negotiable every trip.
Coffee shop, quick grocery run, a walk in the park. Build confidence before attempting a 3-hour Target run.

items.item: Have a backup plan if things go sideways
items.details: Know your exit strategy. If baby melts down, it's totally fine to abandon the cart and leave. No one is judging you.

items.item: Accept that baby might cry in public
items.details: Every parent in earshot has been there. Most strangers are sympathetic, not annoyed. You're doing fine.
A quick photo or 'we're doing great' text keeps everyone calm. Plus you'll want to remember this milestone.

items.item: Bring a change of shirt for yourself
items.details: Spit-up on your shoulder at the grocery store is a rite of passage, but a backup shirt in the car is clutch.

name: First Bath at Home
description: Step-by-step guide to baby's first bath - all the supplies and steps to do it safely and calmly

items.details: A contoured infant tub with a sling insert supports the head and keeps baby from sliding. The kitchen sink with an insert also works great.
One to wrap baby in immediately, one as backup if the first gets wet during the process. Hooded towels keep the head warm.
One for face, one for body, one spare. Baby-specific washcloths are softer than regular ones.
Fragrance-free, hypoallergenic. You only need a tiny amount - a pea-sized drop for the whole body. Less is more with newborn skin.

items.item: Cotton balls for eye and face cleaning
items.details: Dampen with warm water and wipe each eye from inner corner outward. Use a fresh cotton ball for each eye.

items.item: Fresh diaper and clean outfit ready
items.details: Have these laid out and unfolded before you start the bath. Wet naked babies get cold fast - speed matters.
Babies lose body heat fast when wet. Close windows, turn up the heat if needed. A warm room prevents shivering and crying.
Use a bath thermometer or your elbow - it should feel warm, not hot. Wrist testing is unreliable. 100°F max, always.

items.item: Gather everything within arm's reach before starting
items.details: Soap, towels, washcloths, diaper, clothes - all within one arm's length. You can never, ever leave baby unattended in water. Not for one second.

items.item: Sponge bath only until the cord stump falls off
items.details: No submerging in water until the umbilical cord falls off (usually 1-3 weeks). Use a damp washcloth for sponge baths instead.
Pat around it, never scrub it. Let it air dry after sponge baths. It'll look gross and crusty - that's normal.

items.item: Fold diaper below the cord stump
items.details: Fold the front of the diaper down so it doesn't rub or trap moisture against the cord. Some newborn diapers have a cord cutout.

items.item: Wash face first with a damp cloth only
items.details: No soap on the face. Wipe eyes, nose, ears, then cheeks and chin with a warm damp washcloth. Do this before getting the body wet.

items.item: Support head and neck at all times
items.details: Your forearm under the head, hand gripping the far armpit. This is the football hold - practice it before bath day.
Soft circular motions with a tiny amount of baby shampoo. Don't worry about the soft spot - it's tougher than it looks.

items.item: Work top to bottom, front to back
items.details: Neck, chest, arms, hands, belly, legs, feet, then diaper area last. This keeps the cleanest areas from getting contaminated.

items.item: Have a warm towel ready to wrap immediately
items.details: Wrap baby in the hooded towel the second they come out. Pat dry, don't rub. Pay special attention to skin folds - neck, armpits, thighs.
Moisturize with fragrance-free lotion if skin is dry, then diaper and dress fast. The whole process from water to clothed should be under 2 minutes.

name: Newborn Sleep Setup
description: Everything you need to know and set up for safe sleep from day one

items.item: Firm, flat mattress with tight-fitting sheet only
items.details: No memory foam, no toppers, no soft mattresses. The crib mattress should not indent when you press on it. One fitted sheet and nothing else.

items.item: Remove all bumpers, blankets, toys, and pillows from the crib
items.details: Bare crib only. No matter how cute the crib set looks, those items are suffocation hazards. AAP is unambiguous on this.

items.item: Room share but not bed share
items.details: Baby sleeps in their own crib or bassinet in your room for the first 6-12 months. Bed-sharing increases SIDS risk even if you're a light sleeper.

items.item: Always place baby on their back to sleep
items.details: Back to sleep, every sleep, every time. Once baby can roll both ways on their own, you don't need to reposition them.

items.item: No inclined sleepers, swings, or rockers for unsupervised sleep
items.details: Dozens of infant deaths linked to inclined sleepers. The only safe sleep surfaces are flat cribs, bassinets, and play yards.

items.item: Set up bassinet or crib by your bed
items.details: Bassinet is easier for the first 3-4 months since you can reach baby without getting up. Check the weight limit - most cap at 20 lbs.
Velcro swaddles are the easiest. Muslin wraps take practice. Try a few styles - every baby has a preference. Stop swaddling when baby shows signs of rolling.
Volume under 50 decibels, placed at least 7 feet from baby's head. Continuous noise, not nature sounds or lullabies that cycle off.
Darkness signals melatonin production. Cover the window completely - even small light leaks affect naps. Test from inside with lights off.

items.item: Place room thermometer near the crib
items.details: Keep the room between 68-72°F. Dress baby in one more layer than you'd wear. If they're sweating at the neck, they're too warm.
Video with night vision so you can check without opening the door. Audio-only works too, but you'll end up sneaking in to look anyway.
Newborns can only stay awake 45-60 minutes before they're overtired. Watch for yawning, eye rubbing, fussiness - those are the cues.
Put baby down when eyes are heavy but still open. It won't work every time at first - that's normal. You're building a skill, not flipping a switch.

items.item: Establish day vs night difference from week one
items.details: Bright lights, activity, and noise during the day. Dim lights, quiet voices, and boring diaper changes at night. Baby will catch on in 2-4 weeks.

items.item: Keep night feeds dim and boring
items.details: Red or amber light only. No talking, no eye contact, no stimulation. Feed, burp, change, back to bed. The goal is 'this is still nighttime.'

items.item: Agree on a night shift schedule
items.details: Split the night into shifts: 8 PM - 1 AM and 1 AM - 6 AM. Each person gets one uninterrupted 5-hour block. Sleep deprivation wrecks everything.
If bottle-feeding, have bottles pre-made in the fridge (use within 24 hours) or have formula and water measured out. Fumbling at 3 AM is miserable.

items.item: Set up a night feeding station
items.details: Dim lamp, water bottle, burp cloth, extra swaddle, phone charger, snack - all in arm's reach of the glider. You shouldn't need to leave the room.

items.item: Know when to transition from swaddle to sleep sack
items.details: When baby starts showing signs of rolling (usually 3-4 months), switch to a sleep sack with arms free. Cold turkey or one-arm-out method both work.

items.item: Offer a pacifier after feeding is established
items.details: AAP recommends pacifiers at sleep time after breastfeeding is established (around 3-4 weeks). Reduces SIDS risk. Don't stress if it falls out.

name: Postpartum Support Plan for Mom
description: Your game plan to support mom's recovery - physical, mental, and logistical

items.details: Hospital gives one but buy backups. Warm water rinse after every bathroom trip for 2-4 weeks. The upside-down squeeze bottle design is easier to use.
Instant cold packs or DIY with witch hazel pads in the freezer. She'll want these for the first 1-2 weeks. Stock at least a dozen.
The first postpartum bowel movement is genuinely feared. Colace (docusate) daily starting day one post-delivery. This is non-negotiable.
Mesh hospital underwear runs out fast. High-waist disposable or period underwear protects the incision site (C-section) and holds pads in place.
Milk leaks are constant in the first weeks, especially during letdown. Keep extras in every room and the diaper bag.
Apply after every feeding session for the first 2-3 weeks. Cracked nipples are common and incredibly painful. This isn't optional.

items.item: Freeze 2 weeks of meals before the due date
items.details: Soups, casseroles, burritos, pasta bakes - things you can reheat one-handed. Label everything with the date and reheating instructions.

items.item: Set up a meal train with friends and family
items.details: Use MealTrain.com or TakeThemAMeal.com. Coordinate dates so you don't get 5 lasagnas on Monday and nothing by Thursday.
Granola bars, trail mix, cheese sticks, cut fruit, lactation cookies. Mom will be hungry and holding a baby 80% of the time.

items.item: Place water bottles in every room
items.details: Breastfeeding moms need 80-100 oz of water daily. Put a filled 32 oz bottle on every surface she sits - couch, bed, glider, kitchen.

items.item: Learn the warning signs of postpartum depression and anxiety
items.details: Persistent sadness, inability to bond with baby, intrusive thoughts, panic attacks, rage. This is not 'baby blues' - baby blues resolve in 2 weeks.
24/7 helpline, text support, and online support groups. Save this number in both your phones now, not when you need it.

items.item: Identify a therapist who takes your insurance
items.details: Find one who specializes in perinatal mental health. Get the intake paperwork done before baby arrives so it's one call to start sessions.

items.item: Plan daily emotional check-ins with your partner
items.details: Ask 'How are you really doing?' every day and actually listen. A 5-minute honest conversation catches problems before they spiral.

items.item: Understand that postpartum depression affects dads too
items.details: 1 in 10 new fathers experience PPD. Sleep deprivation, identity shifts, and feeling helpless are real. You're allowed to not be okay.

items.item: Line up cleaning help for the first month
items.details: Hire a cleaner, ask a friend, or accept every offer of help. The house will be a wreck and that's fine, but basic cleaning keeps things livable.
Toilet paper, paper towels, dish soap, laundry detergent, trash bags. One less reason to leave the house in those bleary first weeks.

items.item: Arrange pet care if you have pets
items.details: Dog walking, feeding schedule backup, vet info accessible. Your dog doesn't understand why walks got shorter. Line up a friend or service.
Instacart, Amazon Fresh, or your store's delivery. Create a recurring order template with essentials so reordering takes 2 minutes.

items.item: Set visiting hours and communicate them
items.details: 10 AM - 2 PM works for most families. Text it to everyone before baby arrives. 'We love you but we need sleep' is a complete sentence.

items.item: Require flu shot and TDAP for all visitors
items.details: Whooping cough can kill a newborn. Anyone holding baby needs TDAP (ideally 2 weeks before visit) and a current flu shot. No exceptions.

items.item: Designate a gatekeeper for visitor coordination
items.details: Pick one trusted person (sibling, parent, best friend) to manage all visit requests. You and your partner should not be fielding 30 texts about visit times.

name: First Vacation with Baby
description: How to plan, pack, and survive your first trip away from home with a baby

items.details: Skip the 14-hour flight to Bali. Pick somewhere 2-4 hours away with baby-friendly amenities, a kitchen, and a washer/dryer.
Flights, hotels, rentals - all refundable or flexible cancellation. Babies get sick, plans change, and a no-refund policy makes everything worse.

items.item: Check with pediatrician about travel vaccines or restrictions
items.details: Some destinations require specific vaccines. Pediatrician may recommend waiting on certain trips based on baby's age and immunization schedule.

items.item: Research nearest hospital and urgent care at destination
items.details: Find the closest pediatric ER and save the address in your phone. Know your insurance coverage for out-of-network emergency visits.

items.item: Get a letter from your pediatrician for medications
items.details: If traveling with infant Tylenol, prescription meds, or formula, a doctor's note prevents hassles at airport security or border crossings.

items.item: Pack double the diapers you think you need
items.details: For a 3-day trip, that's 30-40 diapers. Travel disrupts routines and that often means more blowouts. You can always bring extras home.

items.item: Bring a portable crib or pack-n-play
items.details: Hotel cribs are unreliable and often unavailable. Your own portable crib means baby sleeps in something familiar with a mattress you trust.

items.item: Bring a travel stroller that fits overhead or gate-checks easily
items.details: Compact umbrella strollers fold small and survive gate-checking. Your full-size jogger is not the move for airports.

items.item: Use a car seat travel bag
items.details: Protects the car seat from damage during flights. Some bags have wheels - worth the extra $20. Check your car seat free on most airlines.

items.item: Pack baby's sound machine and sleep sack
items.details: Recreate the sleep environment from home. Same sound, same sleep sack, same bedtime routine. Consistency is the only thing fighting jet lag.

items.item: Time travel around baby's nap schedule
items.details: Drive during nap time or fly during a sleep window. A well-rested baby handles travel 10x better than an overtired one.

items.item: Bring 2-3 new small toys for distraction
items.details: Dollar store toys they've never seen. The novelty buys you 20-30 minutes of quiet. Ration them throughout the trip.

items.item: Pack an extra outfit for yourself
items.details: Spit-up on a plane with 3 hours left is rough. A clean shirt in your carry-on saves you from arriving at your destination smelling like formula.

items.item: Bring more snacks and formula than you think you need
items.details: Delays happen. Pack enough food and formula for double the travel time. A hungry baby on a delayed flight is everyone's nightmare.

items.item: Feed baby during takeoff and landing
items.details: Swallowing equalizes ear pressure. Nurse or give a bottle during ascent and descent. Pacifiers work too if baby isn't hungry.

items.item: Request a crib from the hotel in advance
items.details: Call ahead, don't rely on the booking note. Confirm it'll be in the room at check-in. Have your pack-n-play as backup.

items.item: Bring outlet covers and do a room safety scan
items.details: Get on your hands and knees and look for hazards: cords, sharp edges, small objects, unsecured furniture. Takes 5 minutes at check-in.

items.item: Check room for hazards at baby level
items.details: Minibars with glass bottles, curtain cords, balcony access, loose lamp cords. Move or block anything within reach before you unpack.

items.item: Download a white noise app as backup
items.details: In case you forget the sound machine or it breaks. Test it before the trip so you know which sounds work. Keep your phone charged.
Morning outings when baby is fresh, back for afternoon nap, easy evening. Fighting the schedule means everyone suffers.

items.item: Alternate adventure days with rest days
items.details: Beach day Monday, pool hangout Tuesday, explore Wednesday, lazy day Thursday. Baby (and you) need recovery time between activities.

items.item: Have indoor backup plans for every outdoor plan
items.details: Rain, heat, unexpected meltdowns - always have a Plan B. Hotel pool, local library, baby-friendly cafe. Flexibility is the whole game.

name: First Overnight at Grandparents'
description: Everything you need to prep, pack, and hand off for baby's first night away from home

items.item: Write out the full daily routine with times
items.details: Wake time, nap windows, meal times, bath time, bedtime. Grandparents need a minute-by-minute playbook, not vibes.

items.item: Document feeding schedule and exact amounts
items.details: Breast milk or formula amounts per bottle, solid food portions if applicable. Include how to warm bottles and what temperature baby prefers.

items.item: Write out the full sleep routine step by step
items.details: Every step matters: diaper, sleep sack, sound machine on, lights off, specific song or phrase. Skip a step and you'll get a 1am phone call.
Dosage, timing, how to administer. Leave the medication in original packaging with a printed label showing the schedule.
Both parents' numbers, pediatrician, poison control (1-800-222-1222), nearest ER address. Stick it on the fridge.

items.item: Pack favorite stuffed animal or lovey
items.details: The one specific comfort object baby reaches for at bedtime. If you don't send it, nobody sleeps.
Whatever baby sleeps in at home. Grandparents defaulting to loose blankets is the #1 safe sleep risk to address.

items.item: Send sound machine or share the app
items.details: White noise is non-negotiable if baby uses it. Portable Hatch or download the same app on grandparent's phone.

items.item: Pack pacifier if baby uses one
items.details: Send at least 2 - one will disappear into couch cushions within the first hour. Include the clip if you use one.
Not for sleeping - for floor time and comfort. A blanket that smells like home helps baby settle in an unfamiliar space.
Count on 8-12 diapers for an overnight. Pack 15 to be safe - running out means a grandparent guessing your diaper brand at Target.
Full pack of wipes plus the specific diaper cream you use. Generic substitutions can cause rashes on sensitive skin.

items.item: Pack 2 full changes of clothes plus pajamas
items.details: Blowouts, spit-up, food mishaps. Two daytime outfits and one set of pajamas is the minimum for an overnight.
Outlet covers, cabinet locks, gate at stairs, cords out of reach. Their house hasn't had a baby in 25+ years - walk through it together.
Firm mattress, fitted sheet, nothing else in the crib. No bumpers, pillows, or quilts. Set it up together and verify it meets current safety standards.
Back to sleep, nothing in the crib, room temperature 68-72°F. This conversation feels awkward but it's the most important one you'll have.
If grandparents will be driving, install the car seat yourself or send a how-to video for their specific vehicle. 80% of car seats are installed wrong.

items.item: Leave a comfort item that smells like you
items.details: Worn t-shirt tucked near (not in) the crib, or a burp cloth you've used. Your scent is the strongest comfort signal baby has.

items.item: Do a trial run with a shorter stay first
items.details: Start with a 4-5 hour stretch before committing to overnight. Builds confidence for everyone - baby, grandparents, and you.

items.item: Agree on photo/video update schedule and have a pickup plan
items.details: Set expectations: one update at bedtime, one in the morning. And agree on the threshold for calling you to come get baby - better to have the plan and not need it.

name: First Birthday Party Planning
description: How to plan a first birthday that celebrates the milestone without losing your mind or your budget

items.item: Set a realistic budget and stick to it
items.details: First birthdays can spiral fast. Set a number - $200, $500, whatever works - and make every decision against it. Baby won't remember any of this.

items.item: Choose venue: home, park, or rented space
items.details: Home is cheapest and easiest for nap time. Parks are great but have no climate control. Rented venues mean no cleanup but higher cost.
Doesn't have to be the exact birthday. Pick a weekend that works for key family members and gives you time to plan without stress.
Keep it manageable - 15-20 guests is the sweet spot. More people means more stimulation for baby and more logistics for you.

items.item: Send invites at least 3 weeks ahead
items.details: Digital invites are fine. Include start time, end time (critical), address, and any allergy info you need from guests with kids.
Small 4-6 inch cake just for baby. Get it in a flavor baby has already tried - not the time to discover a new food allergy on camera.

items.item: Plan an allergen-safe menu for babies
items.details: If other babies are attending, avoid common allergens or clearly label everything. Ask parents about allergies on the invite.
Cut fruit, cheese cubes, puffs, soft crackers. Everything bite-sized, nothing round and hard. Think grab-and-go, not sit-down meal.
Adults need to eat too. Pizza, sandwiches, or a simple taco bar. Easy to serve, easy to eat one-handed while holding a baby.

items.item: Confirm allergy info from all parents attending
items.details: Follow up on your invite RSVP. One missed peanut allergy can turn a birthday party into an ER visit.

items.item: Get a high chair birthday banner
items.details: Simple, photogenic, and keeps baby's area festive. Attach it securely - baby will try to eat it.

items.item: Get a "1" balloon or number display
items.details: Great photo prop and instant party atmosphere. Mylar lasts longer than latex and is less of a choking hazard when it pops.

items.item: Create a monthly milestone photo display
items.details: Print one photo from each month. String them up with mini clothespins. Guests love it and it hits you right in the feelings.

items.item: Keep all decorations out of baby's reach
items.details: Balloons, streamers, confetti - all choking hazards. Hang everything high and skip confetti entirely. Not worth the risk or the cleanup.

items.item: Set up a photo station with props
items.details: Simple backdrop, a few props (party hat, the "1" sign), good lighting. You'll use these photos for years.

items.item: Plan the party around baby's nap schedule
items.details: An overtired birthday baby is a crying birthday baby. Schedule the party during baby's best awake window - usually late morning or mid-afternoon.

items.item: Designate a quiet room for overwhelmed babies
items.details: Noise, strangers, stimulation overload. Have a calm space with a white noise machine where any baby can decompress.
90 minutes is perfect. Two hours is the max. After that, baby is done and adults are ready to leave. End on a high note.

items.item: Charge all cameras and phones the night before
items.details: Dead battery during cake smash is a pain you don't recover from. Charge everything, clear storage space, and have backup power banks.

items.item: Assign someone to take candid photos
items.details: You'll be hosting, not photographing. Ask a friend or family member to be the unofficial photographer so you can actually be present.

items.item: Capture the cake smash on video
items.details: Set up a tripod or prop your phone before cake time. You want both hands free and a stable shot. This is the money moment.

items.item: Get the group photo early, before meltdowns
items.details: First 30 minutes when everyone's fresh. After cake, half the kids will be crying and half the adults will be covered in frosting.

name: Choosing a Pediatrician
description: How to research, interview, and pick the right doctor for your baby before they arrive

items.details: The best pediatrician in town is useless if they're out-of-network. Start by filtering your insurance provider's directory for in-network options.

items.item: Read online reviews but weigh them carefully
items.details: Google, Healthgrades, and Zocdoc reviews give you a vibe check. Look for patterns (always running late, great with anxious parents) rather than one-off complaints.

items.item: Ask friends and family for recommendations
items.details: Parents in your area with kids are your best resource. They'll tell you things no review will - how the office handles a screaming 2-year-old at 5pm on a Friday.
Check the American Board of Pediatrics website. Board-certified means they passed rigorous exams and maintain continuing education. Non-negotiable.
A 45-minute drive with a sick baby at 7am is brutal. Prioritize proximity and look for offices with early morning, evening, or weekend hours.
Babies get sick at 2am, not during business hours. Find out if they have a nurse line, on-call doctor, or partner with an urgent care.

items.item: Ask who covers when the doctor is out
items.details: Solo practitioners take vacations. Multi-doctor practices mean you'll see someone different sometimes. Know the backup plan before you need it.
You want a doctor who follows the AAP/CDC schedule and can clearly explain why. If their answer is vague or wishy-washy, keep looking.

items.item: Ask how they handle urgent phone calls
items.details: Can you call with a quick question, or does everything require an appointment? The difference matters at midnight with a 103°F fever.
15-20 minutes is normal. If they're consistently 45+ minutes behind, that's a practice management issue that won't improve with your baby screaming in the waiting room.

items.item: Observe general cleanliness of the office
items.details: Clean floors, sanitized toys (or no shared toys), wiped-down surfaces. Your newborn's immune system is brand new - the office should reflect that.

items.item: Check for separate sick and well-child waiting areas
items.details: You don't want your healthy newborn sitting next to a kid with the flu during a routine checkup. Separate areas or staggered scheduling is a green flag.
The front desk sets the tone. If they're dismissive or rushed during your visit, imagine calling them panicked about a rash at 4pm.

items.item: Check appointment availability and booking ease
items.details: Can you book online? How far out are well-visits booked? Can they fit in a same-day sick visit? Accessibility matters more than aesthetics.
Carrying a car seat, diaper bag, and stroller into a building with no parking and no elevator is a nightmare. Drive the route and test the parking situation.

items.item: Confirm same-day sick appointments are available
items.details: This is the single most important logistical question. If you can't get a same-day sick visit, you'll end up in urgent care or the ER instead.

items.item: Ask if lab work is done on-site or by referral
items.details: On-site labs mean one trip instead of two. Dragging a sick baby to a separate lab is an extra hour nobody has.

items.item: Check for a patient portal for messaging
items.details: Secure messaging for non-urgent questions saves phone calls and wait times. Photo uploads for rashes are a game changer.

items.item: Confirm newborn visit within 48 hours of hospital discharge
items.details: Most pediatricians want to see your baby 1-2 days after discharge. Confirm they can schedule this before you commit - it's your first real appointment.

items.item: Trust your gut feeling after the visit
items.details: Credentials and logistics matter, but so does the feeling you get. Did the doctor listen? Did you feel heard? Your instinct is data too.

name: First Sports / Activity Class
description: How to choose, prep for, and get the most out of baby's first structured activity class

items.details: Parent-baby swim starts at 6 months, music classes at 3 months, baby gym at 6 months. Match the class to your baby's developmental stage, not your ambition.
Infant swim instructors should have ISR or equivalent certification. Music teachers should have early childhood training. Credentials matter when your baby is involved.

items.item: Read parent reviews for the specific class
items.details: Not the facility - the specific class and instructor. A great gym can have a terrible baby class. Ask in local parent groups for honest feedback.

items.item: Try a free trial class before committing
items.details: Most places offer a free or discounted first class. Never pay for a 10-week session without testing it. Baby might hate it and that's OK.

items.item: Pack swim diaper and regular diaper for swim class
items.details: Swim diaper goes on in the pool, regular diaper for before and after. Bring two of each because one blowout changes everything.

items.item: Bring a full change of clothes
items.details: Wet, messy, or just a surprise blowout. You'll need a complete outfit including socks. Pack it even for a music class.
Post-activity hunger is real. Puffs, a pouch, or crackers and a sippy cup. Fed baby equals happy car ride home.

items.item: Bring a towel for swim or messy classes
items.details: Hooded baby towels are ideal. Facility towels are rough and never warm enough. Your own towel is a comfort signal for baby.

items.item: Apply sunscreen if the class is outdoors
items.details: SPF 50, broad spectrum, applied 15 minutes before class. For babies under 6 months, shade and clothing are better than sunscreen - check with your pediatrician.
Not too full, not hungry. A full stomach in a swim class leads to spit-up. A hungry baby in a music class leads to screaming through every song.

items.item: Pack the diaper bag the night before
items.details: Morning scramble plus class prep equals forgetting something essential. Pack everything the night before and do a 30-second check before you leave.

items.item: Arrive 10 minutes early for the first class
items.details: Paperwork, waivers, finding the room, getting baby changed. Rushing in late with a baby is maximum stress. Early is on time.
Snaps beat zippers, onesies beat full outfits. You'll be changing in a locker room or a corner in approximately 90 seconds.

items.item: Follow baby's lead, not the class schedule
items.details: If baby wants to watch instead of participate, that's OK. If they need a break, take one. Learning happens through observation too.

items.item: It's OK to step out if baby is overwhelmed
items.details: New sounds, new people, new environment - it's a lot. Step out, calm down, try again. No instructor worth their certification will judge you.

items.item: Don't compare your baby to other kids
items.details: The baby doing perfect kicks started 6 months ago. Yours is exactly where they should be. Comparison steals the joy from every activity class.

items.item: Offer a snack or feed immediately after
items.details: Activity burns energy. Baby will be hungry and possibly overtired. Feed first, then head home for a nap.

items.item: Give it 3 sessions before deciding if baby enjoys it
items.details: First class is sensory overload. Second class is adjustment. Third class is the real test. Don't quit after one bad experience.

name: Baby Photo & Memory Keeping
description: A system for capturing, organizing, and preserving baby's first year before the moments slip away

items.item: Buy milestone cards or a milestone blanket
items.details: Monthly number cards or a blanket with months printed on it. Pick one style and stick with it for consistency across all 12 months.

items.item: Use the same spot and time of day each month
items.details: Same chair, same blanket, same window light. The consistency is what makes the 12-month comparison photo hit. Natural light near a window works best.

items.item: Include a size reference item in every photo
items.details: Same stuffed animal, same pillow, or same parent's hands. It shows how much baby has grown in a way numbers can't capture.

items.item: Take the photo on the actual monthly date
items.details: Born on the 15th? Photo on the 15th of every month. Set a recurring calendar reminder or you'll be 3 days late by month 4.

items.item: Shoot in natural light near a window
items.details: No flash, no overhead lighting. Position baby facing a window for soft, even light. Morning light is usually the most flattering.
Tiny socks on the counter, the bottle warmer at 3am, sleeping on dad's chest. These boring moments become the most precious photos in 5 years.

items.item: Set up automatic cloud backup for photos
items.details: iCloud, Google Photos, or Amazon Photos - pick one and enable auto-upload. A dropped phone in month 8 shouldn't erase months 1-7.

items.item: Create a shared album with your partner
items.details: Shared Google Photos album or iCloud shared library. Both parents capture moments - combine them so neither misses the other's perspective.
Usually happens around 6-8 weeks. Keep your phone ready during alert, happy times. The first real smile (not gas) changes everything.
Typically around 3-4 months. Video, not photo - you want the sound. You'll replay this clip hundreds of times.

items.item: Document first roll, first solid food, and first steps with date
items.details: Write the date on your phone immediately or text it to yourself. Memory is unreliable - by month 9 you won't remember if the first roll was month 4 or 5.

items.item: Choose a physical or digital baby book
items.details: Physical books (Mushie, Promptly) feel special but require effort. Digital apps (Tinybeans, Qeepsake) are easier to maintain. Pick whichever you'll actually use.

items.item: Save the hospital bracelet and hat
items.details: Those tiny hospital ID bracelets and the first knit hat are irreplaceable keepsakes. Stick them in a ziplock bag immediately - they disappear into laundry piles fast.

items.item: Keep baby's first outfit or going-home outfit
items.details: Wash it, fold it, and store it in an airtight bag. In 20 years you'll hold it and not believe a human that small existed.

items.item: Press one flower from baby's birth month
items.details: Buy or pick a flower from the month baby was born. Press it in a heavy book for 2 weeks. A small, beautiful keepsake that costs almost nothing.

items.item: Create monthly photo folders on your phone or computer
items.details: Month 1, Month 2, etc. Move photos weekly or you'll have 3,000 unsorted photos by the first birthday and zero motivation to organize them.

items.item: Back up photos to two separate locations
items.details: Cloud + external hard drive, or two different cloud services. One backup is zero backup. The 3-2-1 rule applies to baby photos too.
Pick 10-15 favorites every 3 months and print them. Digital photos stay on phones - printed photos go on walls and into grandparents' hands.

items.item: Get a hand and footprint kit
items.details: Ink pad or clay impression kit. Do it in the first week when hands and feet are impossibly tiny. The clay kits from Amazon work fine - don't overpay.

items.item: Save a lock from baby's first haircut
items.details: Put it in a small envelope or locket. Label it with the date. First haircuts happen around 12-18 months for most babies.

name: Infant CPR & First Aid Prep
description: Training, supplies, and knowledge every parent needs before an emergency happens

items.item: Find an AHA or Red Cross infant CPR class
items.details: Search redcross.org or heart.org for classes near you. In-person beats online - you need to feel the compression depth on a mannequin to get it right.

items.item: Both parents attend the class together
items.details: Both of you need the muscle memory. If one parent is with baby and something happens, there's no time to call the other one for instructions.

items.item: Practice compressions and rescue breaths on an infant mannequin
items.details: Two fingers, 1.5 inches deep, 100-120 compressions per minute. It feels aggressive on something that small. That's normal - practice until it's automatic.

items.item: Learn the Heimlich maneuver for infants
items.details: 5 back blows, 5 chest thrusts, check the mouth, repeat. Different from adult Heimlich - never do abdominal thrusts on an infant under 1 year.

items.item: Plan to refresh your training annually
items.details: Set a calendar reminder for 12 months out. Skills degrade fast without practice. A 1-hour refresher course can save a life.

items.item: Get an infant thermometer (rectal is most accurate)
items.details: Rectal temperature is the gold standard for babies under 3 months. Forehead and ear thermometers are convenient but less accurate when precision matters most.
Safe for babies 2+ months. Know the weight-based dosing before you need it - check with your pediatrician for your baby's specific dose.

items.item: Stock infant ibuprofen for 6 months and older
items.details: Motrin or Advil infant drops. NOT for babies under 6 months. Keep both acetaminophen and ibuprofen so you can alternate if needed during high fevers.

items.item: Get a nasal aspirator and saline drops
items.details: NoseFrida or bulb syringe plus saline drops. Babies can't blow their noses - you'll use this weekly during cold season. Two drops of saline, wait 30 seconds, then suction.
Assorted adhesive bandages, gauze pads, medical tape, and Neosporin or Bacitracin. Minor cuts and scrapes start happening as soon as baby is mobile.

items.item: Save pediatrician's after-hours line in your phone
items.details: Program it as a contact now, not when you're panicked at 2am trying to remember the office number. Label it clearly: 'Dr. Smith After Hours.'
Save it in both parents' phones right now. When baby eats something they shouldn't (and they will), you need this number in 10 seconds, not 10 minutes.

items.item: Save the nearest ER address in your phone's maps
items.details: Star it in Google Maps or Apple Maps as a favorite. Know the fastest route from your house. Practice the drive so it's automatic when adrenaline takes over.

items.item: Identify a trusted neighbor for emergencies
items.details: Someone within 5 minutes who can come watch your other kids or meet you at the house. Exchange keys and phone numbers. Community is the real safety net.

items.item: Learn which foods are choking hazards
items.details: Grapes, hot dogs, popcorn, raw carrots, peanut butter by the spoonful. The AAP has a full list. Print it and tape it to the inside of a kitchen cabinet.

items.item: Always cut grapes and hot dogs lengthwise
items.details: Quarter grapes and cut hot dogs lengthwise, then into small pieces. Round shapes are the exact size and shape of a baby's airway. This is the #1 choking food.

items.item: No popcorn, whole nuts, or hard candy under age 4
items.details: These are firm choking hazards that can't be crushed by baby teeth. It's not being paranoid - it's being informed. The ER sees these cases every week.

items.item: Always supervise eating and know gagging vs. choking
items.details: Gagging is loud with coughing - baby is handling it. Choking is silent with no airflow. If baby is coughing, let them work it out. Silent and red? That's when you act.

items.item: Know the signs that require calling 911 immediately
items.details: Difficulty breathing, lips turning blue, unresponsive, seizure, severe allergic reaction (swelling, hives, trouble breathing), or fall from significant height. Don't hesitate - call first, assess second.

items.item: Learn how to take baby's temperature correctly
items.details: Rectal for under 3 months (lubricate tip, insert 1/2 inch). Armpit works for a quick check on older babies. Know the method before the 2am fever arrives.

items.item: Know normal temperature ranges and fever thresholds
items.details: Normal: 97.8-100.4°F. For babies under 3 months, 100.4°F or higher is an ER visit - no exceptions. For older babies, call the pediatrician at 101°F+.

items.item: Recognize signs of dehydration in infants
items.details: Fewer than 6 wet diapers in 24 hours, no tears when crying, sunken fontanelle (soft spot), dry mouth. Dehydration in babies escalates fast - call the pediatrician immediately.

name: Life Insurance & Will Updates
description: The financial and legal prep most parents put off but every family needs done before baby arrives

items.item: Calculate coverage needed: 10-12x your annual income
items.details: If you make $80k, you need $800k-$960k in coverage. Factor in mortgage payoff, childcare costs for 18 years, and your partner's lost income if they stop working.

items.item: Compare term life vs. whole life insurance
items.details: Term life is cheaper and covers you for 20-30 years - the period your family most needs it. Whole life is more expensive and rarely the right call for young parents. Start with term.

items.item: Get quotes from at least 3 providers
items.details: Policygenius, Haven Life, and Ladder are good starting points for online quotes. Rates vary wildly - a 30-year-old non-smoker can get $500k term for $20-30/month.

items.item: Both parents need coverage, not just the breadwinner
items.details: If the stay-at-home parent dies, the cost of full-time childcare, cooking, cleaning, and household management is $40-60k/year. Both lives have financial value.
Spouse as primary, with a contingent beneficiary (usually a trust for the child). Never name a minor as a direct beneficiary - the court will appoint a guardian for the money.
An online will through Trust & Will or LegalZoom costs $150-300. It's not romantic, but dying without one means a judge decides who raises your kid.

items.item: Name a legal guardian for your baby
items.details: The hardest conversation you'll have. Pick someone who shares your values, has the capacity, and has agreed to the responsibility. Have the conversation before you write it down.
If your first choice can't serve, the court needs a Plan B that you chose. Without one, a judge picks based on whatever petition comes first.

items.item: Consider setting up a living trust
items.details: A trust avoids probate and gives you more control over how money is managed for your child. Worth the $500-1500 cost, especially if you own property.

items.item: Store legal documents securely and tell someone where
items.details: Fireproof safe at home or safe deposit box. Your executor needs to know where to find the will. A will nobody can find is the same as no will at all.
Log into your retirement accounts and update beneficiary designations. These override your will - if your ex is still listed, they get the money regardless of what the will says.
Add payable-on-death (POD) designations to checking and savings accounts. Takes 10 minutes at the bank and ensures money transfers instantly without probate.

items.item: Review and update existing life insurance beneficiaries
items.details: If you had a policy before baby, the beneficiary might be outdated. Log in and verify. This takes 5 minutes and prevents months of legal headaches.
You'll receive the birth certificate 2-6 weeks after birth. Order 2-3 certified copies - you'll need them for insurance, passport, and school enrollment.

items.item: Plan for Social Security card application
items.details: Apply at the hospital when you fill out birth certificate paperwork. Card arrives by mail in 2-4 weeks. You'll need the SSN to add baby to insurance and open a 529.

items.item: Add baby to health insurance within 30 days of birth
items.details: This is a hard deadline - most insurers give you 30 days from birth to add a dependent. Miss it and you wait until open enrollment. Call HR on day 1.
New dependent = lower tax withholding. Submit an updated W-4 to HR to increase your take-home pay. You'll also get the Child Tax Credit ($2,000/year) at tax time.
Tax-advantaged education savings. Even $25/month adds up over 18 years. Most states offer a tax deduction for contributions. Start early - compound interest is your best friend.
If you can't work due to injury or illness, disability insurance replaces 60-70% of your income. Check your employer benefits - most people are underinsured and don't know it.

items.item: Update your emergency fund target to 6 months of expenses
items.details: Baby increases monthly expenses by $500-1500. Recalculate 6 months of expenses with the new number. This fund is the difference between a tough month and a crisis.

name: Paternity Leave Planning
description: How to prepare, protect, and maximize your time off when baby arrives

items.item: Confirm your paternity leave duration and pay
items.details: Check your employee handbook or ask HR directly. Some companies offer 2 weeks paid, some offer 12+ weeks. Know exactly how many days you get and at what pay percentage.
FMLA gives you 12 weeks unpaid (job-protected) if you've worked 12+ months and 1,250+ hours at a company with 50+ employees. Know your eligibility before you plan.

items.item: Check state-specific paid family leave laws
items.details: California, New York, New Jersey, Washington, and others have state-paid leave programs. Some stack on top of employer benefits. Google '[your state] paid family leave' now.

items.item: Review short-term disability options if applicable
items.details: Some employers offer STD that covers bonding time. Check if it applies to non-birthing parents. It's often overlooked free money sitting in your benefits package.

items.item: Document all ongoing projects with status and next steps
items.details: For every project you own, write a one-page summary: current status, next milestone, blockers, and who to contact. Your brain will be mush after day 3 of no sleep.

items.item: Create handoff documents for each project
items.details: Name the person covering each project, walk them through it, and give them everything they need to succeed. A good handoff means fewer calls during your leave.

items.item: Set up auto-responders on email and Slack
items.details: Draft your out-of-office now so it's ready to flip on. Include who to contact for urgent issues, your return date, and a clear statement that you won't be checking messages.

items.item: Brief your backup person or cover
items.details: Don't just send a doc - sit down (or call) and walk them through everything. Answer their questions now so they don't need to call you at 2am during a feeding.

items.item: Discuss your start date with your manager 6+ weeks before the due date
items.details: Give your manager time to plan coverage. Be direct: 'Baby is due [date], I plan to start leave on [date], and here's my transition plan.' Proactive beats reactive.

items.item: Plan for early arrival - babies don't check calendars
items.details: 10-15% of babies arrive before the due date. Have your handoff docs ready by week 36 at the latest. If baby comes early, you don't get a planning grace period.

items.item: Consider splitting leave if your company allows it
items.details: Some dads take 2 weeks at birth and save the rest for when partner returns to work. Splitting covers two critical transition periods instead of one.

items.item: Coordinate your leave with your partner's leave
items.details: Overlapping leave in the first 2 weeks is essential - you both need to learn together. Then stagger remaining leave to extend total coverage before childcare starts.

items.item: Set firm boundaries on work contact
items.details: Tell your team: 'I'm unreachable unless the building is on fire.' Every Slack message you answer trains them to keep messaging. Boundaries are a skill, not a perk.

items.item: Designate one check-in per week maximum if needed
items.details: If you absolutely must stay connected, one 15-minute call per week with your manager. Not daily emails, not 'just a quick question' Slacks. One call, scheduled, boundaried.

items.item: Turn on out-of-office on all platforms
items.details: Email, Slack, Teams, Notion, whatever your company uses. Set it on every single platform. One missed out-of-office status and people think you're available.

items.item: Don't feel guilty about being offline
items.details: Your company existed before you and will exist while you're out. The baby who needs you right now won't be this small ever again. This is the job right now.

items.item: Schedule a gradual return if your company allows it
items.details: Half days for the first week, or 3 days the first week then full the second. Going from 24/7 baby mode to full work mode in one day is brutal on everyone.

items.item: Arrange childcare before your return date
items.details: Daycare waitlists can be 6-12 months long. If you're using daycare, get on the list during pregnancy. If it's family, confirm the schedule weeks ahead.

items.item: Plan a first-week-back buffer with lighter workload
items.details: Block your first week's calendar with 'transition' time. No major meetings, no deadlines. You'll need time to catch up on what happened while you were out.

items.item: Discuss flexible schedule options and update emergency contacts
items.details: Ask about remote days, adjusted hours, or compressed work weeks. Also update HR with your new emergency contacts and any changes to your benefits. One conversation covers both.
